original_abstract:
While political parties are often treated as unitary actors in spatial models, they are internally complex organizations driven by diverse member preferences. This article investigates the causes and consequences of intraparty preference heterogeneity by examining how electoral conditions and governing status influence internal dissent and leadership selection. We theorize that electoral losses and perceived incompetence trigger increased disagreement within a party, as rival factions challenge the dominant leadership's direction. However, we argue that the governing context acts as a stabilizer; party members grant leaders greater ideological flexibility when in power, whereas opposition parties face heightened internal pressure for policy purity—particularly when a strong economy makes the incumbent government appear more competent.

To test these propositions, we present a new dataset of intraparty actor preferences derived from automated text analysis (WORDFISH) of speeches delivered at national party congresses in Germany (CDU, SPD) and France (PS, UMP). The empirical results demonstrate that internal disagreement increases in opposition parties during periods of economic growth, reflecting a perceived failure of the leadership. Furthermore, our analysis of leadership elections reveals that while candidates closer to the party's ideological median generally receive more votes, being in government insulates candidates from the electoral penalties of deviating from that median. By opening the "black box" of intraparty politics through natural language processing, this study highlights the complex interplay between executive responsibility, economic performance, and the internal democratic life of political parties.
humanized_abstract:
In the spatial literature, political parties are often treated as a single actor but here we investigate the roots and implications of intra-party heterogeneity given that parties are pluralistic organisations, home to a wide variety of views. We examine how different election circumstances and governing positions impact the levels of intraparty conflict and party leadership contests. The rationale underlying our investigation is the following: each time a party loses an election and a rival faction contends that the incumbent leader was bad because it (hypothesis a) leads to higher levels of intra-party conflicts; however, in general we suggest that governing is a stabilising factor for parties and this for a couple of complementary reasons: - Under such circumstances, the party members accept a higher degree of ideological margin for their leaders (something that opposition parties are not allowed to do in the model due to higher degree of homogeneity and party discipline caused by electoral pressures) - The more that the ruling party is enabled to exploit a favourable economic context thus leading to higher perceived competence and hence lower levels of conflict.

Testing the theoretical hypotheses requires a new type of data: the intraparty actor preferences that parties can take into account when deciding the representatives and actions to undertake in the political marketplace. We exploit speeches at the national party conferences of the CDU, SPD in Germany and PS, UMP in France and turn them into a new data set on intraparty preferences using the WORDFISH method. We then investigate empirically the conditional dynamics of intraparty disagreement and test its relations with parties' competencies in the political marketplace, thereby shed new light on the black box of the intraparty politics by employing automated content analysis methods. More specifically, our main findings are: 1) internal conflict increases within opposition parties during periods of governmental responsibility and especially in times of economic prosperity; 2) a closer location to the party median — even in leadership contests — increases the electoral success of a candidate, but with one important exception: governments find it easier to elect leaders that deviate more from the median of their parties.
